coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence sensor film for hydroquinone based on a graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon composite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Butwong, Nutthaya
Heng, Sothearoth
Kunawong, Thidarat
Kunthadong, Pimpanitpa
Mukdasai, Siriboon
Uppachai, Pikaned
coredata.dc:description: A novel film sensor, composed of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon, chitosan, and PVA, offers a simple and effective hydroquinone (HQ) detection. This film exhibits impressive HQ adsorption under ambient conditions (room temperature, pH 7, 3 h) and utilizes a readily observable fluorescence color change for quantification. Upon HQ binding, the film's fluorescence color shifts from yellow-green to blue, enabling a linear detection range of 1.0–150 mg⋅L −1 based on intensity analysis of the blue component (B value). Calibration curves generated on the same day (n = 3) exhibit high precision, with standard deviations of the linear equation parameters below 0.014 and a low detection limit of 0.5 mg⋅L −1. The film exhibits exceptional stability, retaining its color and performance for up to 20 days. This stability is further corroborated by recovery experiments utilizing HQ-spiked water samples at concentrations of 2.5 mg⋅L −1 and 40 mg⋅L −1, where recoveries of 106 % and 95 % were achieved, respectively. These results demonstrated the sensor's reliable quantification capabilities. Notably, the sensor exhibited insignificant interference from commonly co-existing substances such as ethanol, ascorbyl glucoside, arbutin, kojic acid, and methylene blue. However, Cu²⁺, Fe³⁺, and catechol did cause some interference. This versatility, coupled with its straightforward operation, makes the film sensor a promising candidate for on-site HQ detection in diverse environmental monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydroquinone sensor
Nano-carbon materials film
Fluorescence film
Graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon composite preparation
Fabrication of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film
Evaluation of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film for hydroquinone detection
Graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film preparation and characterization
Investigation of the adsorption of hydroquinone on graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/ polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan composite films
Application of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan composite film as hydroquinone sensor
Analytical performance of film-based hydroquinone sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: This work was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT): Fundamental Fund [grant numbers FF66-P1-002].Nutthaya Butwong reports financial support was provided by Rajamangala University of Technology Isan and the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT). Nutthaya Butwong has a petty patent for Fluorescence and adsorbent film from carbon nanomaterials and the preparation process is pending (application number 2303001378). No conflict of interest by the reader. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.We are grateful for financial support from the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) and Rajamangala University of Technology Isan (Basic Research Fund: grant numbers FF66-P1-002).

We are grateful for financial support from the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) and Rajamangala University of Technology Isan (Basic Research Fund: grant numbers 180766, 2023 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of GQDs-NAC (Inset scale bar 50 nm) (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of the films as a function of excitation at 320 nm (c) FTIR absorption spectra of films fabricated with distinct components (d) The light transmittance spectra of the films with and without carbon nanomaterials adding.
(a) The swelling behavior of films with various compositions (n = 3) (b) Thermal stability of the films under a nitrogen atmosphere.
SEM image of the film's topography: PVA/CS (a) GQDs/PVA/CS (b) NAC/PVA/CS (c) and GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS (d).

(a) The effect of solution pH on hydroquinone adsorption efficiency by analyzing replicate samples (n = 3) across a range of pH values (b) The impact of initial HQ concentration on adsorption efficiency by treating replicate samples (n = 3) with varying HQ concentrations (c) The influence of adsorption time by treating replicate samples (n = 3) on the initial HQ concentration of 3 mg⋅L −1 for different durations.

(a) The established relationship between B value intensity of the fluorescence intensity of the film (excited at 366 nm) and concentration of HQ after immersing it in HQ solutions of varying concentrations (1.00–500 mg⋅L −1) for 3 h (b) A calibration curve relating the relative B intensity to HQ concentration of the fluorescence response at different HQ concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity of the film, excited by 366 nm UV light.
The selectivity of the film-based HQ (1 mg⋅L −1) optical detection with the influence of potential interfering substances at the same concentration level.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence sensor film for hydroquinone based on a graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon composite

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Applied Physics, Faculty of Engineering, Rajamangala University of Technology Isan, Khon Kaen Campus, Khon Kaen 40000, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel film sensor, composed of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon, chitosan, and PVA, offers a simple and effective hydroquinone (HQ) detection. This film exhibits impressive HQ adsorption under ambient conditions (room temperature, pH 7, 3 h) and utilizes a readily observable fluorescence color change for quantification. Upon HQ binding, the film's fluorescence color shifts from yellow-green to blue, enabling a linear detection range of 1.0–150 mg⋅L −1 based on intensity analysis of the blue component (B value). Calibration curves generated on the same day (n = 3) exhibit high precision, with standard deviations of the linear equation parameters below 0.014 and a low detection limit of 0.5 mg⋅L −1. The film exhibits exceptional stability, retaining its color and performance for up to 20 days. This stability is further corroborated by recovery experiments utilizing HQ-spiked water samples at concentrations of 2.5 mg⋅L −1 and 40 mg⋅L −1, where recoveries of 106 % and 95 % were achieved, respectively. These results demonstrated the sensor's reliable quantification capabilities. Notably, the sensor exhibited insignificant interference from commonly co-existing substances such as ethanol, ascorbyl glucoside, arbutin, kojic acid, and methylene blue. However, Cu²⁺, Fe³⁺, and catechol did cause some interference. This versatility, coupled with its straightforward operation, makes the film sensor a promising candidate for on-site HQ detection in diverse environmental monitoring applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroquinone (HQ), a ubiquitous industrial pollutant, poses significant health risks due to its toxicity and environmental persistence [1]. Therefore, its efficient monitoring and removal from water resources are critical. Although electrochemical sensors offer advantages in HQ detection, limitations such as cost, maintenance, electrode fouling, and interference from other compounds in complex matrices like wastewater necessitate careful consideration [2]. Additional removal techniques like extraction and activated carbon adsorption have been explored, but they often suffer from drawbacks like high costs or slow adsorption times. Therefore, environmental remediation demands the development of efficient strategies that not only effectively remove HQ but also enable their efficient monitoring of its contamination.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising ultrasensitive sensors due to their unique quantum confinement and edge effects. Compared to the other carbon dots, GQDs boast superior sensitivity and diverse sensing capabilities from their rich composite structure and functional groups. Notably, π-π interactions with aromatic molecules and graphene-like electrochemical properties make them versatile electrode materials, finding applications in energy storage and novel fluorescence sensor platforms [3]. Besides, nano-activated carbon (NAC), with its high surface area (> 2000 m²/g), intricate pore structure, and tunable surface chemistry, offers remarkable adsorption capabilities. NAC can capture a wide range of molecules, from small pollutants to large gas molecules, and even allows for selective adsorption based on size [4]. Additionally, its high thermal stability and biocompatibility broaden its potential applications [5]. From environmental remediation and energy storage to drug delivery and toxin removal, NAC is poised to make a significant impact across various scientific and technological fields [6]. The combination of GQDs and NAC offers a synergistic approach for HQ adsorption and detection. GQD's π-π interactions and hydrophobicity, coupled with NAC's high surface area and hydroxyl groups, significantly enhance HQ capture. Crucially, the UV-responsive fluorescence of GQDs enables direct HQ quantification by changing the fluorescence intensity, providing a simple analytical approach.

The limitations of using powdered carbon nanomaterials as adsorbents can be overcome by transforming them into convenient film formats. Chitosan (CS) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were chosen due to the synergistic properties of their blend. The hydrophobic force, aggregation phenomena, ether linkages, and inter/intramolecular bonds created a robust and mechanically stable composite [7]. Notably, the differing solubility of PVA and CS facilitates pore formation within the CS matrix, resulting in a high surface area with accessible hydroxyl groups. These features enhance the composite's overall reactivity. Additionally, the inherent amino groups on CS provide binding sites for negatively charged pollutants in water, promoting efficient adsorption [8]. This innovative film-based approach offers promising applications in environmental and industrial settings for targeted adsorption. Beyond adsorption, the incorporated nanocarbon materials introduced further functionalities to the film. Notably, these materials impart electrical conductivity, making the film a potential candidate for electrochemical applications. They also reduce the film's permeability to gases and water, rendering it suitable for organic molecule adsorption. Furthermore, the nanocarbon materials can modify the film's optical properties such as transparency and color. The specific effects depend on the type, concentration, and processing conditions used [9-11]. For instance, studies have shown increased tensile strength and Young's modulus with MWCNT incorporation, enhanced thermal stability with GO inclusion, and reduced water vapor permeability with carbon black addition.

This study unveils a dual-function material by integrating two carbon nanomaterials within PVA/CS films to adsorb and detect HQ in water. Simultaneously, the GQDs-NAC nanocomposite's unique optical properties enable in-situ HQ quantification via fluorescence quenching, providing valuable real-time insights into contamination levels. This innovative sensor-adsorbent platform provided water purification and continuous monitoring of HQ, contributing significantly to environmental protection and public health safeguarding.

Poly Vinyl Alcohol (M.W. = 100,000), citric acid, sodium hydroxide, ethanol, methylene blue, FeCl3⋅6H2O and Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O were obtained from Chem-Supply™ (Gillman, Australia). While, NAC was obtained from the Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Engineering, Rajamangala University of Technology Isan, Khon Kaen Campus, Khon Kaen, Thailand, using the hydrothermal-microwave radiation technique [12]. At the same time, other reagents including silver nitrate, sodium borohydride, hydroquinone, acetic acid, arbutin, ascorbyl glucoside, kojic acid, and chitosan (medium molecular weight) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA).

A JEOL JSM-6010LV scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) was employed to characterize the surface topography and elemental composition of the film. A FEI/TECNAI G2 20 Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to characterize GQDs and GQDs-NAC. A PerkinElmer STA 600 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) was utilized to assess the thermal decomposition behavior of the composite film, providing insights into its thermal stability and potential applications. The PerkinElmer Spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer was utilized to identify and characterize the functional groups present within the nanoparticles. The optical properties of the nanoparticles and films were investigated at room temperature using a Shimadzu RF-5301 pc spectrofluorometer and a UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer. To quantitatively assess the intensity and color profile of the film's fluorescence (excited at 366 nm), a Xiaomi Mi 10 Pro smartphone equipped with the Color Grab algorithm version 3.9.2 ©loomatix Ltd. was employed. This innovative method facilitated real-time RGB reflectance value measurements, offering a convenient and portable approach for HQ quantification. The smartphone's specifications (Android OS, v4.0.4, 8 MP camera, automatic focusing) and measurement setup (photo light box with UV lights, camera 30 cm distance) were carefully controlled to ensure consistent and reliable data acquisition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon composite preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The desired amount of citric acid (within the 2–4 g range) was weighed and transferred into a clean beaker and pyrolysis at 200 ± 5 °C for 10 min. The gradual color transformation from colorless to reaching a deep orange color indicated successful GQDs formation, then carefully introducing 50.0 mL of 1.0 M NaOH solution to quench the reaction and stabilize the GQDs. Then, an appropriate amount of NAC was added to the obtained GQDs colloidal while proper dispersion by stirring the mixture at a constant temperature of 90 ± 5 °C for 3 h to promote efficient interaction and integration between GQDs and NAC. Before precipitation, the pH of the solution was adjusted to neutrality using dilute acid or base addition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film
ce:section.ce:para: The solutions of PVA and chitosan were prepared separately by dissolving each polymer in deionized water and 1 % (v/v) acetic acid, respectively. A mixture of 2 % (w/v) of PVA and chitosan 2 % (w/v) was stirred together to ensure complete dissolution and homogeneity. Then, the GQDs-NAC composite was added to the prepared polymer mixture by gentle stirring to ensure uniform integration and prevent aggregation. The glycerol (0.15 mL) was added to the mixture and stirred at 40 °C. The homogenous mixture was carefully poured onto a clean glass Petri dish, ensuring a uniform film thickness. The film was solidified and any remaining solvent residues were removed by placing the Petri dish in a vacuum oven preheated to 60 °C and allowing the solvent to evaporate completely. Once the film has dried and cooled, cut it into desired squares using clean tools and store the film pieces in a brown zip-lock plastic bag for protection from light and moisture until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film for hydroquinone detection
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of HQ onto graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan (GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS) composite films was investigated under controlled conditions to assess their effectiveness in HQ detection using a fluorescence-based approach. Films were sectioned into 1 × 1 cm squares, weighed, and pre-treated by soaking in deionized water for 30 min to achieve the maximum swelling before the adsorption of HQ. Subsequently, films were immersed in HQ solutions/samples for 3 h, allowing for equilibrium adsorption. Following exposure, films were irradiated with UV light (366 nm), and their fluorescence was captured photographically. Color Grab application was then employed to quantify the films' fluorescence intensity, expressed as the B value in the Red Green Blue (RGB) color system, at varying HQ concentrations within the detection range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan film preparation and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The properties of the synthesized GQDs and GQDs-NAC composites were characterized using various techniques, and the results are presented in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 (Supplementary File). Notably, FTIR and TEM analyses confirmed the desired composition of the GQDs successful incorporation within the NAC. TEM images of GQDs-NAC were evaluated (Fig. 1 a), confirming the GQDs-NAC is uniformly nano-dispersed. They range in diameter from 3 to 5 nanometers.

The influence of GQDs loading on the performance of GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS composite films was investigated. Constant amounts of PVA and chitosan were employed at 2 % (w/v), while the GQDs content was varied by adjusting the amount of citric acid used as a precursor during GQD synthesis. Citric acid concentrations of 2, 3, and 4 g were studied, as increasing citric acid generally leads to higher GQDs yields. The experimental results (Fig. S3a) demonstrated that films could be successfully prepared at all GQDs loadings. The color of the films darkened with increasing GQDs content, reflecting the higher GQDs concentration. The prepared films were evaluated for their ability to adsorb HQ at an initial concentration of 2 mg⋅L −1. Films were cut into 1 × 1 cm pieces and immersed in HQ solution. The results (Fig. S3) revealed that GQDs prepared using 3 g of citric acid yielded films with the highest HQ adsorption efficiency, exceeding 98 %. Increasing the citric acid concentration beyond 3 g did not further enhance HQ adsorption. Therefore, 3 g of citric acid was chosen as the optimal citric acid concentration for GQDs synthesis, as it resulted in films with superior HQ adsorption performance.

To determine the optimal NAC content for film preparation, GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS composite films were synthesized using different NAC amounts (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 g). In comparison, GQDs were prepared using 3 g of citric acid. The HQ adsorption efficiency was evaluated for each NAC loading. As depicted in Fig. S3b, increasing the NAC content resulted in darker films with reduced flexibility of the film. The HQ adsorption efficiency increased with increasing NAC content up to 0.1 g and was then relatively stable. This observation is attributed to the well-known adsorptive properties of activated carbon. Since further NAC addition did not significantly enhance HQ adsorption and negatively impacted the film's light efficiency, 0.1 g of NAC was selected as the optimal amount for film preparation. This choice yielded films with an HQ adsorption efficiency exceeding 99 %.

Fig. 1 highlights the optical properties of the GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS composite film (b and d). Interestingly, the mixture of GQDs and NAC exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity at 320 nm excitation, exceeding films containing only GQDs. This suggests synergistic interactions between GQDs and NAC, promoting efficient energy transfer and enhancing fluorescence emission (Fig. 1b). NAC's structure keeps GQDs well-dispersed, preventing aggregation and dimming fluorescence. Conversely, pristine GQDs displayed peak fluorescence at the same excitation wavelength, indicating their inherent luminescence properties. Furthermore, the addition of NAC to GQDs likely played a role in their dispersion within the composite film, preventing aggregation and maximizing light-emitting capabilities, thus leading to superior brightness.

The light transmission analysis further reveals significant differences between the composite films and pristine PVA/chitosan film. Notably, composites exhibited prominent transmittance in both the UVA and visible light ranges, unlike PVA/CS films devoid of nano-carbon materials, which only allowed light passage in the visible regime. This behavior can be attributed to the functional groups present in the film components, some of which possess UV-absorbing properties [13]. However, GQDs/PVA/CS composites still permitted some light passage through the UVA range, unlike films containing only NAC, which blocked UV light entirely (Fig. 1d). This differential transmittance suggested that GQDs within the composite contribute to enhanced light absorbance in the UVA range of the film [14]. Exposing the GQDs-NAC/CS/PVA film to UV light at 366 nm resulted in a green-blue glow, confirming its inherent optical properties. This luminescence further verifies the successful integration of GQDs-NAC within the composite film, opening up potential applications in areas like optoelectronics and biosensing.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra confirmed the presence of expected components within the composite films (Fig. 1c). Peaks at 2922 cm⁻¹ and 3282 cm⁻¹ corresponded to -CH stretching and hydrogen bond vibration in PVA, respectively. The peak at 1725 cm⁻¹ indicated the degree of PVA hydrolysis (C = O stretching). Characteristic peaks for chitosan were observed around 825 cm⁻¹ (glucopyranose ring), 1640 cm⁻¹ and 1368 cm⁻¹ (CN stretching), and 1028–1060 cm⁻¹ and 1158 cm⁻¹ (CO stretching and glycosidic linkage) [14]. The presence of nanocarbon materials in the composite films was evident by the fluctuation in peak intensity between 1200 and 1750 cm⁻¹, suggesting an interaction with chitosan/PVA [15]. This interaction likely involved amine bonds (C = N) around 1560–1548 cm⁻¹. Additionally, characteristic peaks for the specific nanocarbon material were identifiable, depending on its type [16].

The swelling behavior of the films was investigated due to the presence of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a well-known hydrogel material known for its water absorption properties. The swelling ratio was employed to evaluate the films' water uptake capacity, a crucial parameter for effective HQ adsorption. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the swelling ratio increased with the addition of GQDs to the film. These results demonstrated that GQDs promoted water molecule penetration into the polymer matrix, leading to enhance swelling [17]. Conversely, incorporating NAC either alone or combined with GQDs, resulted in a decrease in swelling compared to the PVA/chitosan film. This phenomenon can be attributed to the increased crosslinking of polymer chains induced by the presence of NAC [18]. The swelling index data indicated that the films reached their maximum swelling capacity within 30 min. Consequently, films were soaked in deionized (DI) water for 30 min before HQ detection experiments.

To characterize the thermal stability of PVA/CS films with different amounts of GQDs and NAC, TGA thermograms were obtained under nitrogen (N2) conditions in the temperature range of 50–640 °C (Fig. 2b). The thermal stability of the film can be separated into three phases. Initial weight loss (50–270 °C) due to dehydration of water molecules. Significant weight loss (290–430 °C) influenced by the added material [19]. Oxidation or carbonization of organic residues (310–510 °C), leaving carbonized residues of the film in the aluminum container [16]. The overall thermal degradation behavior for the films was the same. However, the weight loss behavior was slightly different for the NAC-added films. The relative thermal stability of the film increased as presented by NAC in the nanocomposite film. This suggests that GQDs-NAC acts as a thermal barrier component of the film [20]. Additionally, the thermal stability of the film was improved due to the elevated melting point induced by the nanocarbon materials.

SEM revealed the impact of nanocarbon material incorporation on film morphology (Fig. 3 ). The PVA/CS film with GQDs (Fig. 3b) displayed a smooth surface consistent with the expected morphology of GQDs. Conversely, the film containing only NAC (Fig. 3c) exhibited particle aggregation. Interestingly, the film incorporating both GQDs and NAC (Fig. 3d) showed a more uniform distribution of nanocarbon materials. This suggests a synergistic interaction between GQDs and NAC, promoting their homogeneous dispersion compared to using them individually. The observed dispersion pattern can be attributed to the interplay between the hydrophilic nature of GQDs and the hydrophobic character of NAC. The composite of nanomaterials with hydrophilic and hydrophobic carbon nanomaterials within the film provided a cross-linking effect. Moreover, the incorporation of nanocarbon materials likely enhanced the mechanical properties of the PVA/CS film, potentially acting as reinforcing agents that improve strength and resistance to deformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the adsorption of hydroquinone on graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/ polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on HQ adsorption by the GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS composite films was investigated within a range of 3–8 (Fig. 4 a). Adsorption efficiency exhibited a distinct pH-dependent trend, progressively increasing from pH 3 to 4 and reaching a maximum of 80 % at pH 4. This behavior is likely due to the combined effects of surface charge on the composite film and the ionization state of HQ molecules. At acidic pH (3–4), abundant hydrogen ions protonate the surface of GQDs, creating positively charged functional groups. These positive sites electrostatically attract HQ molecules, which possess Lewis's basic character due to their hydroxyl groups and lone electron pairs [20]. This interaction facilitates efficient adsorption. As the pH increases beyond 4, HQ transitions to its phenolate anion form, acquiring a negative charge and hindering electrostatic interactions with the neutral or slightly negative NAC surface [21]. Additionally, the electron redistribution within the phenolate anion might weaken its Lewis basicity, further reducing adsorption affinity. Consequently, the overall adsorption efficiency of HQ onto the composite film declines at higher pH values. Therefore, based on the observed trend, pH 4–7 represents the optimal range for HQ detection using these composite films.

To assess the adsorption capacity and saturation behavior of the GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS composite films, the effect of initial HQ concentration was investigated (Fig. 4b). Square-shaped films (1 cm x 1 cm, ∼0.2 g) were shaken for 60 min in 50 mL solutions containing varying HQ concentrations (0.5–7.0 mg⋅L −1). Adsorbed HQ was quantified using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to determine adsorption efficiency. The observed trend revealed a non-monotonic relationship between initial HQ concentration and adsorption efficiency. As expected, efficiency increased with increasing concentration up to 3.0 mg⋅L −1, reflecting the driving force of the concentration gradient on mass transfer. Interestingly, a slight decrease in efficiency occurred at the highest concentration (7.0 mg⋅L −1). This deviation from a linear trend can likely be attributed to the swelling behavior of the film due to PVA. At high HQ concentrations, the film might experience increased internal swelling due to osmotic pressure, potentially limiting accessible adsorption sites and hindering further uptake. Consequently, the film reaches a saturation plateau near 3.0 mg⋅L −1, with a maximum adsorption capacity of approximately 3 mg⋅L −1 for a 0.2-gram film. Further increases in HQ concentration do not significantly contribute to total adsorption.

The influence of contact time on HQ adsorption was investigated to understand the film's adsorption kinetics (Fig. 4c). Films were pre-soaked in deionized water for 30 min before immersion in HQ solutions. As expected, both adsorption efficiency and specific adsorption amount increased with increasing contact time. Initially, a rapid rise in adsorption was observed, likely driven by the significant concentration gradient between the solution and the film surface. This gradient generates a strong driving force for HQ molecules to rapidly migrate toward the adsorbent. Subsequently, the rate of increase in specific adsorption slowed down, suggesting a transition towards a diffusion-controlled regime. This phase involves the slower diffusion of HQ molecules from the bulk solution onto the film surface and further penetration into its internal pores. The observed gradual rise might indicate limitations in the diffusion kinetics of HQ within the film structure.

The analysis of the relative fluorescence intensity versus HQ concentration (Fig. S4) suggests a combination of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms for HQ binding to the film. This implies that both collisional encounters (dynamic quenching) and the formation of non-fluorescent complexes (static quenching) contribute to the fluorescence signal reduction [22]. Furthermore, the observed effectiveness suggests that synergistic interactions, including π-π stacking, n-π interactions, hydrogen bonding, and pore filling, play a key role in the overall binding mechanism of HQ on the film. [23,24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of graphene quantum dots-nano activated carbon/polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan composite film as hydroquinone sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of GQDs-NAC/PVA/CS composite films as HQ sensors was explored. Following exposure of films (1 × 1 cm) to HQ solutions of varying concentrations (0.05 to 1.2 mg⋅L −1) for 3 h, a statistically significant decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing HQ concentration (Fig. S4). This concentration-dependent quenching suggests the promising potential of these films for HQ quantification. Furthermore, the relative fluorescence intensity of the films (excited at 320 nm) exhibited an exponential relationship with the HQ concentration within the studied range. To further assess the fluorescence properties, the prepared films were evaluated under ultraviolet (UV) light excitation at 366 nm. Notably, the pristine films displayed a distinct blue-green fluorescence, which shifted towards a yellow-green hue upon immersion in water for 30 min (Fig. S5). This intriguing color change can be attributed to the interaction of water molecules with the π-electrons within GQDs [23]. This interaction alters the energy levels of the excited states, consequently modifying the emission wavelength and resulting in the observed shift. Additionally, water molecules may facilitate the aggregation of GQDs, potentially leading to reduced fluorescence intensity compared to the initial dry state. These results demonstrate the stability and reliability of the prepared films as HQ sensors, suggesting their potential for long-term applications in HQ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of film-based hydroquinone sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the film-based HQ sensor was evaluated through investigations of linearity, accuracy, selectivity, and limit of detection (LOD). Linearity was assessed within a broad concentration range of 1.00–500 mg⋅L −1. A strong linear relationship between fluorescence quenching (B value) and HQ concentration was observed within the range of 1.00–150 mg⋅L −1 (Fig. 5 a). Beyond this range, minimal changes in fluorescence intensity indicated the overloading of adsorption sites. To determine accuracy, triplicate measurements of a standard curve were constructed using HQ concentrations from 1.00 to 150 mg⋅L −1 (Fig. 5b) were performed, and their colors were obtained using the Color Grab application (Fig. S6, see supplementary information). The resulting curve displayed a consistent slope and y-intercept. The obtained correlation coefficient (R² > 0.98) exceeded the acceptable threshold for sensor-based screen measurements (Table 1S), demonstrating reliable quantitative analysis within the linear range. LOD was determined using the 3 s/n method, where the HQ concentration was progressively decreased until a visually identifiable B color difference appeared in the film's fluorescence (Fig. S7). This method yielded an estimated LOD of 0.5 mg⋅L −1, indicating the sensor's potential for sensitive HQ detection.

The practical application of the film-based HQ sensor necessitates the evaluation of potential interferences from structurally similar molecules. The influence of ascorbyl glucoside (ASC), arbutin, catechol, and kojic acid was investigated on the sensor's response to HQ (Fig. 6 ). Additionally, potential interferences from methylene blue, ethanol, Cu²⁺, and Fe³⁺ were examined. Interestingly, ASC, arbutin, and kojic acid, despite possessing chemical functionalities related to HQ, did not cause significant interference under these conditions. This might be attributed to the specific functionalities of these molecules that prevent competitive binding. Conversely, catechol, an isomer of HQ with higher reactivity, displayed a distinct impact on the sensor's response, suggesting potential cross-reactivity with the sensing mechanism. The presence of metal ions (Cu²⁺ and Fe³⁺) also affected the fluorescence response of the film compared to HQ alone. This can be explained by the ability of the composite material (GQDs, NAC, and chitosan) to form complexes with these metal ions due to the presence of functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups, which act as Lewis acid-base sites. However, it is important to note that the tested concentration of metal ions (1 mg⋅L −1) is significantly higher than what might be encountered in real-world applications. In addition, the film can adsorb methylene blue without affecting the fluorescence of the film. The findings from the interference studies suggest the possibility of tailoring the film's properties for application in diverse sample matrices. This could be achieved by strategically adjusting the composition of the incorporated GQDs and NAC within the film. The sensor film's accuracy was evaluated by recovery experiments using HQ-spiked deionized water samples (Fig. S8). Recovery experiments using HQ-spiked water samples (2.5 and 40 mg⋅L −1) yielded 106 % and 95 %, respectively.

Remarkable long-term stability was observed in the film's fluorescence. Films immersed in deionized water for 20 days (Fig. S9) retained their color and intensity without significant decline. Even after 30 days in a 50 mg⋅L −1 HQ solution, they maintained bright fluorescence, indicating suitability for long-term monitoring. Visual inspection revealed no signs of degradation or structural issues after 30 days of immersion. This exceptional stability can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the activated carbon nanomaterial and the robust crosslinking within the GQDs-NAC composite film.

This study unveils a novel carbon nanomaterials composite added to PVA/CS film for simple HQ detection and removal. Highlighting strong fluorescence quenching, reliable recovery rates, and minimal interference, the sensor surpasses conventional thresholds. Its simple preparation, extended stability, and visual readout via UV-induced B value offer a practical tool for on-site HQ monitoring. This approach addresses the limitations of conventional methods and offers a cost-effective, environmentally friendly solution for HQ remediation.
Funding: This work was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT): Fundamental Fund [grant numbers FF66-P1-002].

Nutthaya Butwong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sothearoth Heng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Thidarat Kunawong: Methodology, Formal analysis. Pimpanitpa Kunthadong: Validation, Supervision. Siriboon Mukdasai: Visualization, Data curation. Pikaned Uppachai: Resources, Data curation.

Nutthaya Butwong has a petty patent for Fluorescence and adsorbent film from carbon nanomaterials and the preparation process is pending (application number 2303001378). No conflict of interest by the reader. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
